one_line_takeaway: CREDIT defends MLaaS models against extraction by adding Gaussian noise to embeddings and verifying ownership through mutual information against a certified threshold, claiming the first provable error bounds plus 100% CIFAR-10 verification AUC at a fraction of baselines' verification time.
key_finding: CREDIT preserves utility while achieving essentially perfect ownership verification and far lower verification cost than watermarking/fingerprinting baselines. On CIFAR-10 (Table 2), CREDIT attains 100.0 AUC across all four backbones, versus the best baseline results of at most ~80 (e.g., Backdooring 58.64/51.85/74.07/80.25; UAP 52.47/67.28/72.22/79.63 on ResNet/VGG/DenseNet/GoogLeNet). Utility loss is small: on CIFAR-10 ResNet, CREDIT reaches 94.67% vs a 95.70% vanilla model, the best among all defenses (Backdooring 78.10, EWE 90.25, IPGuard 91.08, UAP 84.73). Efficiency is a standout: CREDIT preparation is 0.001s and verification 22.23s (VGG-16) / 22.62s (ResNet-50), versus 72-189s verification for baselines that must train auxiliary models. Certification analysis shows the optimal noise level sigma* = 0.27, with a usable tolerance range sigma in [0.25, 0.45]; increasing sigma raises the false-alarm rate gamma1 but lowers the missed-detection rate gamma2.
core_contribution: CREDIT is presented as the first framework for CERTIFIED ownership verification of DNNs against Model Extraction Attacks (MEAs) in the MLaaS setting. Rather than embedding watermarks or extracting fingerprints, the paper builds a defended model g by injecting independent Gaussian noise Z ~ N(0, sigma^2 I_d) into the target model's embeddings, then uses mutual information (MI) between the embeddings of a suspicious model and the defended model to decide ownership. Its three claimed contributions are: (1) formally formulating certified ownership verification against MEAs and introducing CREDIT; (2) using MI (estimated via the KSG nearest-neighbor estimator) as a principled dependency metric and defining a theoretically grounded 'CREDIT threshold' tau with derived probabilistic error bounds; and (3) an empirical evaluation across image and graph modalities and multiple backbones showing it beats watermarking/fingerprinting baselines. The theoretical backbone is Theorem 3.1 (a Gaussian-mechanism MI upper bound beta = Delta^2/(2 sigma^2) * n on the MI between any model's embeddings and g's), Theorem 3.2 (this bound is information-theoretically tight), the CREDIT threshold tau = beta[1 - rho*exp(-Q*beta/(eta*d*|V|))] in [0, beta], and Theorem 3.4 giving exponentially-decaying-in-|V| bounds on the two error types.
threat_model: MLaaS deployment where a legitimate owner exposes model f via a query API. Adversary mounts a Model Extraction Attack: repeatedly queries the (defended) model g through black-box access to collect input-output pairs and trains a surrogate model h_sur that replicates f's functionality at low cost. Two concrete attack strategies are evaluated: knowledge distillation (adversary minimizes divergence between surrogate predictions and target outputs) and the Knockoff framework with random/unlabeled-data querying. The defender's goal is post-hoc ownership verification: given a suspicious model h (which is either an independently trained model h_ind or an extracted surrogate h_sur), reliably decide whether it was extracted from g, with certified bounds on both Type I (Independent False Alarm) and Type II (Surrogate Missed Detection) errors. A query budget Q is assumed and query and verification sets are kept strictly disjoint.
limitations: Reviewer-inferred from the paper's own text: (1) The headline verification AUC of 100.0 is reported only on CIFAR-10 in the main text (Table 2); comparable per-backbone verification AUC tables for CIFAR-100, ENZYMES, and PROTEINS are not shown in the main body, so the certified-detection claim is empirically demonstrated on a narrow slice. (2) Evaluated MEA strategies are limited to knowledge distillation and Knockoff random querying; adaptive adversaries aware of the Gaussian-embedding defense (e.g., denoising, embedding-alignment, or fine-tuning attacks) are not evaluated. (3) The theory hinges on the global l2 sensitivity Delta of the embedding extractor, whose estimation in practice is not detailed and could be loose, affecting how tight beta and tau really are. (4) The utility bound uses a zero-mean Gaussian approximation of the clean embedding distribution, an admitted simplification. (5) Verification requires white-box-style access to the suspicious model's embeddings to estimate MI, a stronger assumption than pure black-box output access. (6) On some graph datasets CREDIT does not top vanilla utility and trails the vanilla baseline (e.g., ENZYMES/GCN 40.61 vs 42.78), so utility preservation is not uniform.

quantitative_results: Ownership verification AUC on CIFAR-10 (Table 2): CREDIT ('Our') = 100.0 on ResNet, VGG, DenseNet, GoogLeNet; best baselines much lower (Backdooring 58.64/51.85/74.07/80.25; EWE 51.24/62.96/48.77/62.35; IPGuard 40.74/61.11/67.90/50.62; UAP 52.47/67.28/72.22/79.63)
Utility accuracy CIFAR-10 ResNet (Table 1): CREDIT 94.67 vs Vanilla 95.70, Backdooring 78.10, EWE 90.25, IPGuard 91.08, UAP 84.73 (CREDIT best among defenses)
Utility accuracy CIFAR-100 ResNet (Table 1): CREDIT 79.90 vs Vanilla 80.48, IPGuard 77.54, EWE 75.85, Backdooring 74.43, UAP 64.30
Graph utility ENZYMES/GraphSAGE (Table 1): CREDIT 46.94 vs Vanilla 50.83, RandomWM 42.22, BackdoorWM 43.61, SurviveWM 10.27, ImperceptibleWM 27.78

Efficiency (Table 3): CREDIT preparation 0.001s and verification 22.23s on VGG-16 (15M) / 0.001s and 22.62s on ResNet-50 (25.6M); baseline verification 72-189s (e.g., Backdooring 83.10/188.8s, UAP 72.74/180.3s)
Certification: optimal sigma* = 0.27; tolerance range sigma in [0.25, 0.45]; example error rates gamma1 = 0.365, gamma2 = 0.038; at sigma* threshold tau* = 0.59 with MI upper bound beta = 3.58
MI upper bound (Theorem 3.1): beta = Delta^2/(2 sigma^2) * n, shown tight in Theorem 3.2; error probabilities gamma1, gamma2 decay exponentially in verification set size |V| (Theorem 3.4)
baselines_compared: EWE / Entangled Watermarks (Jia et al., 2021) - watermarking, CV
